Scouting Summary

Common themes

  • Left Fast, averaging 129.3 kph (up to 137.3).
  • Usually takes the new ball (opens in 52% of innings).
  • Typical length is 7-8m (their good length); median 7.3 m.
  • Stock ball is a good length wide outside off, swinging away, seaming both ways, passing outside off (19% of deliveries).
  • Goes round the wicket 40% of the time against right-handers.
  • Round the wicket he shifts his pitching line from stumps to wide of 6th.
  • Across an over he varies his length a lot, banging it in more as the over goes on.

Biggest threats

  • Most dangerous length is Full (5 wkts, 2.6% adjusted strike).
  • Danger pitching line: Wide of 6th.
  • Takes 31% of wickets pitching a good length wide outside off.
  • Beats the bat 5.9% of tracked balls (false-shot 10.4%).
  • Gets you caught behind more than most — 88% of his wickets (1.3× the typical rate for this type).
  • 71% of catches are behind the wicket (keeper / slips / gully); most often to keeper, slip: 3rd, cover: short extra.

Areas to exploit

  • Concedes most runs pitching a good length wide outside off (18% of runs).
  • Short ball: 32% of deliveries, economy 3.8 — 4 wkts from 323 short balls.
  • Away from full, the wicket threat drops off — look to score in the less-productive lengths.
  • Most of his runs leak straight down the ground (42%) — his main scoring release.

Solid line = career, dotted = last 3 years (where enough recent balls). Percentile within same-type peers (grey = the peer distribution, line = this bowler). Release/crease vs hand × pace/spin; movement/speed/repeatability vs pace/spin. Release & speed are modern-era (2017+ / partial). Crease variation = how much he moves his release point sideways across the crease from ball to ball (within his main angle): a high percentile = he varies where he lets the ball go a lot, a low percentile = he releases from the same spot every ball. Repeatability = length consistency over his stock-length band (2–11 m, so deliberate yorkers and bouncers don't count as poor control): a high percentile = tighter, more metronomic lengths than peers; a low percentile = he varies his length more. A marker at the very edge = he sits beyond the typical peer range on that trait.

Stock Ball & Ball Types (vs right-handers) ▶ watch stock balls

Stock ball — a good length wide outside off, swinging away, seaming both ways, passing outside off (19% of deliveries, economy 3.02). Minor variations: a good length on the stumps (16%) and a good length down the leg side (10%).
Ball type (pitch length × pitching line)Movement%EconWktsBeaten %
a good length wide outside off ▶swinging away, seaming both ways19%3.02512%
a good length on the stumps ▶swinging both ways, seaming back16%1.4039%
a good length down the leg side ▶swinging both ways, seaming back10%2.30115%
a good length outside off ▶swinging both ways, seaming both ways9%1.0909%
full on the stumps ▶swinging both ways, seaming back8%5.0118%
full wide outside off ▶swinging away, seaming both ways7%4.3516%
Ball type = pitch-length band × pitching-line region. Movement = swing in the air + seam/turn off the pitch, whichever is material, dominant first (ball-tracking, tracked balls only; blank if too few). Beaten % = false-shot rate on tracked balls. The stock-ball line above also notes where it passes the stumps.

Scoring Profile (vs right-handers)

44% of his runs come in boundaries — a boundary every 18 balls; most runs go to straight down the ground (42%); the drive hurts most (23 fours/sixes at 2.5 runs/ball).

Pitch Maps & Scoring

At the Stumps (wickets)
Wicket balls pass the stumps mostly at 6th stump — pitches around wide of 6th and moves it back.
Where They're Scored Off
Runs come mostly on the leg side (63%).
Where They Pitch It
Pitches it a good length wide outside off most often (18%); drops short often (31%).
Where Wickets Come From
Wickets come mostly from balls pitched a good length wide outside off, but strikes most often pitching wide outside off.

Speed & Spells

He is ~3.5 km/h quicker in later spells, is quicker in the 2nd innings, and is as quick on the final day as day one.
Speed by Spell
By spell — opening burst vs later spells.
Speed by Innings
1st vs 2nd innings of the match.
Speed by Day
By match day — fatigue across the game.

Over vs Round the Wicket (vs right-handers)

Round the wicket his pitching line moves from stumps to wide of 6th — economy 2.86→3.30, a wicket every 68→59 balls (over→round).

Sequencing & Over Construction

Variable with his length (length spread ±2.5 m; more repeatable than 54% of pace bowlers); tends to bang it in later in the over (short 6%→11% by the 5th–6th ball); and holds the same crease position through the over.
Across 19 dismissals, the wicket ball lands about 28 cm shorter than the ball before it — that's the change that brings the wicket.
Ball in overMedian lengthShort %EconWkt %

Ball 67.4 m13%2.910.0%
Length spread = std-dev of pitch length (smaller = more repeatable). The table shows how his length, scoring and wicket rate shift across the six balls of an over. Set-up lines compare each ball with the previous delivery in the same over (career, all batters).

Release Point & Crease Use

A high release point (taller than 76% of left-arm pace); releases about 54 cm from the middle stump; varies his spot on the crease a lot (more than 80% of left-arm pace). Comes wider round the wicket (54 cm over vs 89 cm round).

Movement (vs the average pace bowler · vs right-handers)

A hit-the-deck bowler who bangs it into a hard length for steep bounce.
Generates about average seam and about average swing for a pace bowler; seams it in more to right-hand batters (58%).
Swing by ball age to right-hand batters: new ball (≤25 ov, n=339) 54% away / 46% in; old ball (≥40 ov, n=232) 70% away / 30% in.
Percentile vs all Test pace bowlers; direction is which way it moves to this batter. Bounce = extra bounce vs expected.

Beats the bat most at Good Length / Wide of 6th (22% of play-and-misses). Overall he beats the bat 5.9% of tracked balls (false-shot 10.4%, n=1,021).
Test-match career data. Beaten/false-shot use ~38% shot-quality tracking; turn/drift ~30% ball-tracking. Catches split by fielding position (DeliveryFielders view); some catches have no recorded position. Danger zones use empirical-Bayes shrinkage (≥3 wkts to qualify).
